query: inheritance pattern of hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia

retrieved_docs.title: Hereditary Hemorrhagic Telangiectasia (HHT) -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Affected individuals must receive education and counseling on the implications of a possible or definitive diagnosis of HHT. Given that HHT is an autosomal dominant disease, it is essential to screen family members and provide genetic counseling before conception. Early diagnosis before patients are symptomatic can allow for screening tests and interventions to be done promptly and assist in diagnosing at-risk family members. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Hereditary Hemorrhagic Telangiectasia (HHT) -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasias (HHT) is an autosomal dominant bleeding disorder caused by malformed vessels. The condition manifests as telangiectasias affecting the skin and mucosa and arteriovenous malformations involving organs like the lung, brain, and liver. This activity reviews the pathophysiology of the disease and emphasizes the importance of an interprofessional team’s approach to evaluating and treating patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Hereditary Hemorrhagic Telangiectasia (HHT) -- History and Physical -- Family History
retrieved_docs.content: In patients with a positive family history of HHT, the presence of a visceral AVM essentially confirms the diagnosis since AVMs are rare in the general population. For unaffected patients with a parent with HHT, the disease cannot be ruled out due to variable age-related onset of signs and symptoms. European studies on HHT patients estimate the probability of clinical HHT in patients with an affected family member to range from 0.5, 0.22, and 0.01 at 0-, 16-, and 60 years. [7] [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Hereditary Hemorrhagic Telangiectasia (HHT) -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: HHT affects 1 in 5,000-8,000 individuals and can affect both genders and people of all races. [2] [5] [6] [7] Some studies have noted a higher incidence in women, although this gender difference may be attributed to access to healthcare resources. [16] [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Hereditary Hemorrhagic Telangiectasia (HHT) -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the pertinent history and physical exam findings associated with HHT and additional tests to diagnose the condition. Assess the pathophysiology of HHT and its clinical manifestations. Differentiate the medical treatment options that are available for HHT. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Hereditary Hemorrhagic Telangiectasia (HHT) -- Differential Diagnosis -- Hereditary Benign Telangiectasia
retrieved_docs.content: Hereditary benign telangiectasia is an autosomal dominant primary telangiectasia disorder with the development of telangiectasias on the skin and lips during birth or childhood. Lesions are usually asymptomatic and do not have systemic involvement. Unlike in HHT, histology of hereditary benign telangiectasia demonstrates preserved skin and dilated vessels with thicker capillary walls, explaining the lack of hemorrhage. [53]

retrieved_docs.title: Hereditary Hemorrhagic Telangiectasia (HHT) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Although autosomal dominant, the clinical manifestations of HHT are variable, even within families. [6] The classic triad consists of epistaxis, telangiectasias, and positive family history of similarly affected individuals.

retrieved_docs.title: Hereditary Hemorrhagic Telangiectasia (HHT) -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Genes comprise DNA, which encodes for distinct proteins that perform a specific function in the body. Through transcription, the DNA is converted into messenger RNA (mRNA) and then translated into a protein. HHT is an autosomal dominant disorder affecting blood vessels within multiple organ systems. The condition is suspected of haploinsufficiency, where 1 functional gene copy cannot produce the required protein to preserve function. [2] In 97% of patients with a definite clinical HHT diagnosis, a causative mutation is identified in 1 of 3 genes described below. [8] These mutations disrupt signaling by transforming growth factor-beta (TGF-B), essential for maintaining vascular integrity. [9] [10] [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Hereditary Hemorrhagic Telangiectasia (HHT) -- History and Physical -- Mucocutaneous Telangiectasias
retrieved_docs.content: Telangiectasias of the skin and oral mucosa appear around the 3rd decade and increase in number and frequency with age. [6] Recurrent GI bleeds manifest later in 15-20% of HHT patients. [7] Patients should be queried about bleeding frequency and severity. On physical exam, HHT patients have telangiectasias involving the nasal mucosa, oral cavity including hard palate, tongue, and lips, as well as cutaneous lesions on the fingers and nose (see Images. HHT Telangiectasias on Thumb, Lip Telangiectasia). [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Hereditary Hemorrhagic Telangiectasia (HHT) -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Click here for a simplified version. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Hereditary Hemorrhagic Telangiectasia (HHT) -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Early diagnosis of HHT is based on clinical findings. Genetic testing helps confirm the diagnosis but is not required for an index case unless 1) they are children who have not developed all the clinical features or 2) to test at-risk family members and identify a family-specific mutation. Screening and treatment of visceral AVMs depend on the potential for high-risk complications. For instance, proactive screening and treating asymptomatic pulmonary AVMs are encouraged compared to asymptomatic hepatic or cerebral AVMs. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Hereditary Hemorrhagic Telangiectasia (HHT) -- History and Physical -- Classification Criteria
retrieved_docs.content: The diagnosis of HHT has relied on Curaçao Criteria, which encompasses the classic features of the disease: 1) spontaneous, recurrent epistaxis, 2) positive family history, 3) cutaneous or mucosal telangiectasias, and 4) visceral lesions. A definitive diagnosis is made if patients have 3 of the 4 criteria, and a possible or suspected diagnosis is made if patients meet 2 of the 4 criteria. [5] Of note is that the criteria have a poor negative predictive value in children under the age of 16 years. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Hereditary Hemorrhagic Telangiectasia (HHT) -- Etiology -- HHT Type 2 (HHT2)
retrieved_docs.content: HHT2 results from Activin A receptor-like type I (ACVRL1) mutations, which codes for the protein activin receptor-like kinase 1 (ALK1) on chromosome 12. [13] ALK1 is a type 1 cell-surface receptor for the TGF-B superfamily and is found on the surface of cells, particularly on the lining of developing arteries. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Hereditary Hemorrhagic Telangiectasia (HHT) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia (HHT), formerly Osler-Weber-Rendu, is an inherited (autosomal dominant) disease that results in malformed blood vessels (see Image. Telangiectasia on the Tongue). The disease is named after the physicians who first independently described the condition: Henri Jules Louis Marie Rendu in 1896, William Osler in 1901, and Frederick Parkes Weber in 1907. [1] The malformations typically manifest as mucocutaneous telangiectasias and visceral arteriovenous malformations (AVMs). [2] These vascular malformations are responsible for much of the clinical bleeding associated with this disease, ranging from mild epistaxis to life-threatening intracranial bleeds. [3] [4] [5] Some patients with HHT develop pulmonary hypertension, a prothrombotic state, or immune dysfunction. [6] The earliest clinical sign of HHT, often occurring by the second decade of life, is recurrent epistaxis. Telangiectasias, which are dilated blood vessels, are frequently present on the skin and buccal mucosa in the third decade of life. The number of telangiectasias increases with age, accompanied by increased frequency of epistaxis or gastrointestinal (GI) bleeds, leading to anemia, poorer quality of life, and increased healthcare resource utilization, including iron or blood transfusions and hospitalizations. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Hereditary Hemorrhagic Telangiectasia (HHT) -- Differential Diagnosis -- Generalized Essential Telangiectasia
retrieved_docs.content: Generalized essential telangiectasia is rare; inherited and sporadic cases have been reported. The telangiectasias first appear on the lower extremities and slowly spread to involve the entire body. [52]

retrieved_docs.title: Hereditary Hemorrhagic Telangiectasia (HHT) -- Etiology -- HHT Type I (HHT1)
retrieved_docs.content: HHT1 stems from mutations in the gene ENG, which codes for endoglin on chromosome 9. [12] Endoglin is a membrane glycoprotein part of the tumor growth factor-beta (TGF-B) receptor complex needed for vascular integrity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2198
retrieved_docs.content: Hereditary disorder of nervous system is a broad category of genetic conditions affecting the nervous system, and شقيقة فالجية عائلية (familial hemiplegic migraine) is a specific instance within this category. شقيقة فالجية عائلية represents a hereditary form of migraine that is characterized by transient hemiplegia and other neurological symptoms, illustrating how a hereditary disorder of the nervous system can manifest as a distinct migraine subtype.

Collectively, these hierarchical and cross‑disciplinary relationships organize diagnoses by linking specific entities such as congenital vascular dilatation, late cerebrovascular sequelae, and rectal pathology to their larger disease families, underscoring the importance of integrated surgical, pathophysiological, pathological and virological perspectives in patient care.

Clinically, شقيقة and شقيقة فالجية عائلية are considered clinically similar, and شقيقة and صداع وعائي are described as related and possibly synonymous, suggesting substantial overlap in their clinical presentation and underlying mechanisms. Together, these relationships integrate migraine into a network of genetic, vascular, and systemic neurological conditions, underscore its diagnostic and therapeutic considerations, and illustrate its connections to both broader headache classifications and specific hereditary migraine phenotypes.

Overall, migraine (شقيقة) is interconnected with hereditary, vascular, and structural cerebral disorders, is classified across several hierarchical and synonymous relationships, and serves as both a specific clinical entity and a bridge to broader central nervous system disease concepts, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, genetic counseling, and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1214
retrieved_docs.content: Autosomal dominant inheritance is the mode of transmission for فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ (hyperlipidemia type IIa). This genetic pattern means that a single copy of the altered gene inherited from either parent is sufficient to cause the disorder. فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ is characterized by markedly elevated low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels, which increase the risk of premature atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Because the condition follows an autosomal dominant pattern, it often appears in successive generations, and first‑degree relatives of an affected individual have a 50 % chance of inheriting the same lipid abnormality. Recognizing the autosomal dominant inheritance of فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ is essential for family screening, early diagnosis, and implementation of lipid‑lowering therapies to reduce cardiovascular risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1975
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism encompass conditions such as فرط البروتينات الشحمية (hyperlipoproteinemia). Within this broader category, فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ (type IIa hyperlipidemia) is a specific manifestation that is mapped to the class of lipid‑metabolism disorders. Type IIa hyperlipidemia follows an Autosomal dominant inheritance pattern, meaning that Autosomal dominant inheritance is the genetic transmission mechanism for this particular disorder. Recognizing that فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ is a form of فرط البروتينات الشحمية and is inherited autosomally dominant helps clinicians diagnose, screen families, and manage patients with lipid metabolism abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 194
retrieved_docs.content: Migraine (شقيقة) is represented in the knowledge base as a specific nervous‑system disorder that resides within a hierarchy of headache and neurological conditions, being a child of broader entities such as “Headache; including migraine,” “Migraine and Headache Syndromes,” and the general category “اضطراب الجهاز العصبي” (nervous‑system disorder). It is also classified as a type of vascular headache (صداع وعائي) and is closely related to, and sometimes synonymous with, that category. Familial hemiplegic migraine (شقيقة فالجية عائلية) is recorded as a hereditary form of the disorder, linking migraine to genetic and hereditary nervous‑system diseases. Clinically, migraine may appear as a manifestation of other neurological diseases, for example in cerebral arteriopathy, autosomal dominant, with subcortical infarcts and leukoencephalopathy (type 1), where it co‑occurs with gait disturbance. Patient histories noting “H/O: migraine” are mapped to personal histories of nervous‑system and

Overall, migraine occupies a pivotal position at the intersection of pain, headache syndromes, vascular and hereditary neurological disorders, and psychosomatic health, highlighting its multifaceted impact on diagnosis, management, and patient outcomes.

An eye‑related subtype, “الشقيقة الشالة للعين,” is recorded as a child of شقيقة and as a descendant of the cranial‑nerve disorder “اضطراب بالعصب القحفي,” reflecting a specific manifestation that involves ocular symptoms. Genetic and hereditary forms are captured by “Hereditary disorder of nervous system,” which is inversely related to the familial migraine entity “شقيقة فالجية عائلية.” These two migraine variants are noted as clinically similar, underscoring their shared pathophysiology despite possible differences in inheritance patterns. In the context of cerebrovascular disease, the rare condition “CEREBRAL ARTERIOPATHY, AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT, WITH SUBCORTICAL INFARCTS AND LEUKOENCEPHALOPATHY, TYPE 1” lists شقيقة as one of its manifestations, linking migraine to subcortical infarcts and leukoencephalopathy.
